good.label: Good
good.healthImpact: Air quality is satisfactory. Air pollution poses little or no risk.

moderate.label: Moderate
moderate.healthImpact: Air quality is acceptable. Pollutants may affect unusually sensitive people.
moderate.recommendation: Unusually sensitive people should consider reducing prolonged outdoor exertion.

sensitiveGroup.label: Unhealthy for sensitive groups
sensitiveGroup.healthImpact: Sensitive groups may experience health effects. The general public is not affected.
sensitiveGroup.recommendation: Children, elderly, pregnant women and people with cardiorespiratory diseases should limit outdoor exertion.

unhealthy.label: Unhealthy
unhealthy.healthImpact: Everyone may begin to experience health effects. Sensitive groups may experience more serious effects.
unhealthy.recommendation: Avoid prolonged or intense outdoor physical activities. Sensitive groups: stay indoors.

veryUnhealthy.label: Very Unhealthy
veryUnhealthy.healthImpact: Health alert: everyone may experience more serious health effects.
veryUnhealthy.recommendation: Avoid any outdoor activity. Wear a mask if you need to go out. Keep windows closed.

hazardous.label: Hazardous
hazardous.healthImpact: Health emergency: everyone is affected. Serious risk of complications.
hazardous.recommendation: Stay indoors. Do not go outside. Seek medical attention if you feel symptoms.

pm25.description: Solid or liquid particles with aerodynamic diameter less than 2.5 micrometers (µm). So small they penetrate deep into the lungs, reaching the alveoli, and can enter the bloodstream.
pm25.sources: Biomass burning (wildfires), diesel vehicles, industries, thermal power plants and atmospheric reactions of other pollutants.
pm25.effects: Respiratory diseases (asthma, bronchitis), cardiovascular diseases, lung cancer and premature death. Children, elderly and pregnant women are the most vulnerable groups.
pm25.shortDesc: Fine particles (< 2.5 µm). Penetrate deep into the lungs causing respiratory and cardiovascular diseases.

pm10.description: Particles with aerodynamic diameter less than 10 µm. Retained in the upper airways (nose, pharynx, trachea) and bronchi, without reaching the alveoli like PM2.5.
pm10.sources: Dust from unpaved roads, construction, mining, wildfires and soil resuspension.
pm10.effects: Eye, nose and throat irritation, worsening of asthma and bronchitis, respiratory infections and decreased lung function.
pm10.shortDesc: Inhalable particles (< 10 µm). Irritate upper airways and worsen asthma and bronchitis.

no2.description: Reddish-brown gas with a sharp, biting odor. An important component of photochemical smog and a precursor to tropospheric ozone and nitrate aerosols.
no2.sources: Combustion in vehicle engines (especially diesel), thermal power plants and industrial processes.
no2.effects: Airway inflammation, worsening of asthma, increased susceptibility to respiratory infections and increased risk of cardiovascular diseases.
no2.shortDesc: Nitrogen dioxide. Emitted by vehicles and industry. Worsens asthma and increases infection risk.

o3.description: Secondary pollutant formed by the chemical reaction between NOx and Volatile Organic Compounds (VOCs) under sunlight. Unlike the "good ozone" in the stratosphere that protects us from UV radiation.
o3.sources: Not directly emitted — formed in the atmosphere from vehicle, industrial and solvent emissions under intense sunlight.
o3.effects: Eye, nose and throat irritation, cough, difficulty breathing, worsening of asthma and chronic lung diseases. Effects are worse on hot, sunny days.
o3.shortDesc: Tropospheric ozone. Formed by NOx and VOC reactions under sunlight. Irritates eyes and lungs.

co.description: Colorless and odorless gas produced by incomplete combustion of organic materials. Binds to hemoglobin with 200× greater affinity than oxygen, reducing the blood's oxygen-carrying capacity.
co.sources: Motor vehicles (largest urban source), wildfires, furnaces, wood-burning stoves and combustion equipment without adequate ventilation.
co.effects: At low concentrations: headache, dizziness, weakness and nausea. At high concentrations: loss of consciousness and death. Risk groups: heart patients and pregnant women.
co.shortDesc: Carbon monoxide. Odorless, colorless gas that reduces the blood's ability to carry oxygen.

high.label: High
high.recommendation: Reduce exposure between 10am–4pm. Hat and sunscreen.

extreme.label: Extreme
extreme.recommendation: Stay in shade. Risk of sunburn in minutes.

moderate.label: Moderate
moderate.recommendation: Allergy sufferers may experience mild symptoms.

high.label: High
high.recommendation: Moderate to severe symptoms. Take preventive medication.

veryHigh.label: Very High
veryHigh.recommendation: Avoid outdoor exposure if you have allergies.

who.name: WHO – Global Air Quality Guidelines (2021)
who.description: Global reference for PM2.5, PM10, NO₂, O₃ and CO limits. Updated in 2021 with stricter criteria.

inpe.name: INPE – Fire Monitoring
inpe.description: Heat focus and wildfire data detected by satellites over Brazilian territory.

cetesb.name: CETESB – Air Quality in São Paulo State
cetesb.description: Environmental agency of São Paulo State. Brazilian reference in air quality monitoring.

iqair.name: IQAir – World Air Quality Report
iqair.description: Global platform for real-time air quality data and annual country reports.

epa.name: EPA – AirNow (USA)
epa.description: US Environmental Protection Agency. Basis of the AQI index calculation used internationally.

ibama.name: IBAMA – Air Pollution Control Program
ibama.description: Brazilian Institute of Environment and Renewable Natural Resources — environmental enforcement.

cityDashboard.noAlerts: No active alerts. Good air quality.
cityDashboard.noData: Data not available for this city.
cityDashboard.uvTooltipDesc: Measures the intensity of solar ultraviolet radiation. High values increase the risk of sunburn and skin damage.
cityDashboard.pollenTooltipDesc: Concentration of pollen grains in the air. Can trigger respiratory allergies and worsen asthma.

healthAlerts.good_0: Good air quality. No restrictions for outdoor activities.
healthAlerts.moderate_0: Moderate quality. Very sensitive people may feel slight discomfort.
healthAlerts.sensitive_0: Unhealthy for sensitive groups: children, elderly and asthmatics should limit outdoor exertion.
healthAlerts.sensitive_1: Healthy people are generally not affected.
healthAlerts.unhealthy_0: Unhealthy air quality. Avoid intense outdoor physical activities.
healthAlerts.unhealthy_1: At-risk groups: stay indoors with windows closed.
healthAlerts.veryUnhealthy_0: Very unhealthy air quality. Health risk for the entire population.
healthAlerts.veryUnhealthy_1: Children, elderly and cardiorespiratory patients: do not leave home.
healthAlerts.hazardous_1: The entire population should avoid any outdoor air exposure.

ranking.title: NATIONAL RANKING
ranking.subtitle: Air quality across all monitored cities
ranking.noResults: No cities found with the selected filters.

glossary.title: AIR QUALITY GUIDE
glossary.subtitle: Understand the indexes, acronyms and metrics used on the platform.
glossary.aqiSubtitle: Air Quality Index / Índice de Qualidade do Ar
glossary.aqiDesc1: The AQI (Air Quality Index) is a standardized index that transforms concentrations of multiple atmospheric pollutants into a single number from 0 to 500. The higher the value, the worse the air quality and the greater the health risk. Developed by the EPA (US Environmental Protection Agency) and adopted internationally with adaptations.
glossary.aqiDesc2: The index is calculated based on pollutants: PM2.5, PM10, O₃, NO₂ and CO — the final value corresponds to the pollutant with the highest concentration relative to its limit.
glossary.pollutantsSubtitle: Monitored compounds and their health effects
glossary.uvDesc: The UV Index measures the intensity of ultraviolet radiation reaching Earth's surface. High values increase the risk of sunburn, eye damage and skin cancer.
glossary.pollenDesc: The pollen level indicates the number of pollen grains per cubic meter of air. High concentration can trigger respiratory allergies, rhinitis and worsening of asthma in sensitive people.
glossary.footer: RespirA · AirBR — data continuously updated via INPE, IQAir, CETESB and partners.

firemap.title: WILDFIRE MAP
firemap.subtitle: Active foci · INPE/BDQueimadas
firemap.activeFires: active fire foci are affecting air quality in {{cities}} cities

oms.allCompliant: All monitored cities are within the WHO limit
oms.footer: WHO PM2.5 limit: {{limit}} µg/m³ · WHO Air Quality Guidelines 2021
oms.tooltipDesc: The World Health Organization recommends PM2.5 concentration below 5 µg/m³ (annual mean).
oms.tooltipCompliant: This city is within the recommended limit.
oms.tooltipNonCompliant: The PM2.5 concentration exceeds the WHO recommended limit.
